1000.name: First Star

0053.description: The skin of a ripe fig, purple-brown under a dusty bloom.

0054.name: Alizarin
0054.description: The red of madder root, and the first natural dye ever synthesised in a lab (1868); beloved of watercolourists since.

0055.name: Carmine
0055.description: Cochineal's concentrated crimson, once traded at more than its weight in silver.

0056.name: Cranberry
0056.description: Bog-grown and sharp: the sauce, not the juice.

0057.name: Rubine
0057.description: Printer's process red from the rubine pigment family; magenta's punchier cousin.

0058.name: Dark Raspberry
0058.description: Raspberries at the bottom of the punnet, crushed a little.

0060.name: Cerise
0060.description: French for cherry, English for a pink that means business.

0061.name: Rhodamine
0061.description: The fluorescent dye that makes safety pink glow under UV, beloved of microscopists and highlighter engineers.

0062.name: Ruby
0062.description: Corundum with chromium in it: the red that made crowns worth stealing.

0063.name: Rosewood
0063.description: The rose-scented hardwood of guitar fretboards.

0064.name: Crimson Lake
0064.description: A 'lake' is a dye fixed onto powder to make paint; this one is cochineal crimson, a watercolour-box staple.

0065.name: Rose Red
0065.description: The rose of valentines, one shade before cliché.

0066.name: Antique Ruby
0066.description: A ruby that has been in the family long enough to have stories.

0067.name: Yearning Desire
0067.description: Magenta-red pitched exactly at the temperature of wanting something you shouldn't.

0068.name: Fuchsine
0068.description: The 1858 aniline dye named after the fuchsia flower; magenta's original trade name.

0069.name: Punch
0069.description: The bowl at the party: red fruit, questionable additions.

0070.name: Incarnadine
0070.description: Shakespeare's verb for turning things blood-red: 'the multitudinous seas incarnadine'.

0071.name: Cinnapink
0071.description: Cinnamon dust settling on pink icing.

0072.name: Rouge
0072.description: The compact in every 1940s handbag, cheek-red by way of Paris.

0073.name: Watermelon
0073.description: The flesh an inch in from the rind, where the sweetness starts.

0074.name: Old Rose
0074.description: A rose pressed in a book by someone's grandmother.

0075.name: Rosella
0075.description: The crimson-splashed Australian parrot, and the hibiscus cordial that borrows its name.

0076.name: Quail
0076.description: The soft rosy grey-brown of quail plumage.

0077.name: Roseine
0077.description: An early trade name for magenta dye, taken from the rose.

0078.name: Radicchio
0078.description: The bitter chicory whose leaves are wine-red veined with white.

0079.name: Opium
0079.description: A den's haze: mauve-grey and heavy-lidded.

0080.name: Strawberry
0080.description: Ripe at the tip, red all the way through if you're lucky.

0081.name: Punk Pink
0081.description: Pink with the safety pin still in it.

0082.name: Carnation
0082.description: The buttonhole flower; 'carnation' and 'incarnadine' share a root meaning flesh.

0083.name: Erythrine
0083.description: From the Greek erythros, red: an old mineralogist's word for cobalt bloom.

0084.name: Neapolitan
0084.description: The stripe where the strawberry third meets the rest of the block.

0085.name: Hot Pink
0085.description: Pink past the thermostat's limit; the shade that made shocking pink jealous.

0086.name: Wild Strawberry
0086.description: The tiny woodland kind — more perfume than fruit.

0087.name: Hot Lips
0087.description: The kiss print left on the mirror.

0088.name: Solferino
0088.description: Named for the 1859 battle whose carnage inspired the Red Cross, and, in the same year, a magenta dye.

0089.name: Hindu Lotus
0089.description: The sacred pink lotus of South Asia, rising unstained from muddy water; a symbol of purity across traditions.

0090.name: Flamingo
0090.description: Pink on one leg: the bird dyes itself with the carotenoids in its shrimp dinner.

0091.name: Begonia
0091.description: The waxy bedding flower that blooms in relentless pink.

0092.name: Eosin
0092.description: The pink half of every microscope slide's H&E stain, named for Eos, goddess of dawn.

0093.name: Pink Clay
0093.description: Rosy kaolin, the pink of spa masks.

0095.name: Ultra Pink
0095.description: Pink dialled past what ordinary screens were built for.

0096.name: Guava
0096.description: The blushing flesh inside an unassuming green rind.

0097.name: Hyperbloom
0097.description: A flower bed rendered at 120 frames per second.

0098.name: Peony
0098.description: The flower that opens too generously for its own stem.

0099.name: Highlighter Pink
0099.description: The pink marker, for passages you'll definitely read again.

0100.name: Wallflower
0100.description: The quiet dusty pink at the edge of the dance floor; also a real flower that thrives in old walls.

0101.name: Phlox
0101.description: From the Greek for flame: a wildflower that burns pink.

0102.name: Peaches of Immortality
0102.description: In Chinese myth, the peaches in Xi Wangmu's garden ripen once every few thousand years and grant eternal life. Rosy fruit worth the wait.

0103.name: Strawberry Taffy
0103.description: Pulled candy, strawberry flavour: pink stretched until it goes pale at the folds.

0104.name: Roseate
0104.description: The poet's adjective for rose-tinted, as in spoonbills and outlooks.

0105.name: Dayglo Pink
0105.description: Fluorescent poster pink borrowed from the Day-Glo palette of 1960s gig flyers.

0106.name: Bubblegum
0106.description: The first bubblegum's colour — pink only because its inventor had a single dye on hand in 1928.

0107.name: Marabou
0107.description: The feather trim on a showgirl's robe, dyed pink.

0108.name: Rhodolite
0108.description: The raspberry-pink garnet, from the Greek for rose stone.

0109.name: Azalea
0109.description: The shrub that turns whole hillsides pink each spring; Japan and Korea hold festivals under it.

0110.name: Fruity Licious
0110.description: A pink that tastes like the artificial flavour simply called 'fruit'.

0111.name: Petal Pink
0111.description: The inside of a petal, near the stem, where the colour is shyest.

0112.name: Upstream
0112.description: The direction salmon insist on: pink against the current.

0113.name: Cotton Candy
0113.description: Spun sugar: mostly air, entirely childhood.

0114.name: Motel Sign
0114.description: Neon pink buzzing VACANCY at the edge of the highway.

0115.name: Pink Blush
0115.description: The blush you can't will away.

0116.name: Poodle Skirt
0116.description: 1950s felt in sock-hop pink, appliquéd dog included.

0117.name: Shell Pink
0117.description: The inside of a seashell held up to the light.

0118.name: Pink
0118.description: The canonical light red; English only settled on 'pink' as a colour word in the late 1600s, borrowing the flower's name.

0119.name: Gossamer Pink
0119.description: Spider silk at sunrise, pink you can see through.

0120.name: Dark Garnet
0120.description: Garnet at the bottom of the jewellery box: all depth, almost no light.

0121.name: Dark Mahogany
0121.description: The boardroom table after a century of polish.

0122.name: Sang de Boeuf
0122.description: 'Ox blood': the perilous copper-red glaze Chinese potters perfected in the Kangxi era.

0123.name: Oxblood
0123.description: Deep red leather with brown intentions; the classic boot colourway.

0124.name: Cabernet
0124.description: The structured grape: tannin you can see.

0125.name: Dragon's Blood
0125.description: A real resin from Dracaena trees, sold as incense and varnish since antiquity: allegedly dragon, actually tree.

0126.name: Maroon
0126.description: From the French marron, chestnut: the dark red of school uniforms everywhere.

0127.name: Pomegranate
0127.description: The fruit you open like a geode; garnet seeds by the hundred.

0128.name: Brick Dust
0128.description: What's left on your hands after a day of laying reds.

0129.name: Redwood
0129.description: Sequoia heartwood, the red of two-thousand-year patience.

0130.name: Sanguine
0130.description: Blood-red in Latin, cheerful in temperament; the old theory of humours let it be both.

0132.name: Dark Crimson
0132.description: Crimson after the theatre lights go down.

0133.name: Cherry Bomb
0133.description: The firework and the Runaways song, both this red.

0134.name: Chestnut
0134.description: Conker-shell red-brown, fresh from the husk and briefly glossy.

0135.name: Cardinal
0135.description: The red of the songbird and the robe; the bird was named after the cleric, not the other way round.

0137.name: Hot Lava
0137.description: Molten rock where the crust splits, a red that means don't.

0138.name: Madder Lake
0138.description: Madder root fixed into a painter's lake pigment; the old masters glazed shadows with it.

0139.name: Auburn
0139.description: Red-brown hair colour, from the Latin alburnus, 'whitish', which drifted over centuries to mean nearly the opposite.

0140.name: Cherry Red
0140.description: The cherry of jukeboxes and electric guitars rather than orchards.

0141.name: Barn Red
0141.description: The classic barn, red because iron-oxide paint was the cheapest thing that lasted.

0142.name: Lipstick Red
0142.description: The bullet in the golden tube, a red engineered for confidence.

0143.name: Electric Crimson
0143.description: Crimson with the current switched on.

0144.name: Ghost Pepper
0144.description: The bhut jolokia: a million Scoville, a red you feel before you taste.

0145.name: Amaranthine
0145.description: Unfading, like the mythical amaranth flower: a red-pink that refuses to wilt.

0146.name: Weathered Red
0146.description: A door that was scarlet thirty summers ago.

0148.name: Rosy Brown
0148.description: Brown that remembers being pink; an X11 classic.

0149.name: Grenadine
0149.description: The pomegranate syrup at the bottom of a tequila sunrise — sinks first, blushes upward.

0150.name: Safranin
0150.description: The biologist's counterstain; it turns cell nuclei this alarming red under the microscope.

0151.name: Racing Red
0151.description: Rosso corsa by another name: the national racing colour Italy claimed before sponsors claimed everything else.

0152.name: Firebird
0152.description: The phoenix of Slavic folklore, and the Pontiac with one painted across the bonnet.

0153.name: Fire Engine Red
0153.description: Painted this red so nobody can claim they didn't see it coming.

0154.name: Fireweed
0154.description: The first flower to bloom on burnt ground, magenta-red rising out of ash.

0155.name: Sriracha
0155.description: The rooster-bottle hot sauce; a red that escalates.

0156.name: Red
0156.description: The first colour language names after black and white, in culture after culture: blood, fire, stop.

0157.name: Marsala
0157.description: The fortified Sicilian wine, and a divisive Pantone Colour of the Year 2015.

0158.name: Signal Red
0158.description: The exact red of official warnings, engineered to be seen, not admired.

0159.name: Spritz
0159.description: Aperitivo hour in a glass: bitter orange-red over ice, best before dinner.

0160.name: Nipple
0160.description: Anatomically honest rosy brown; the colour list's life-drawing class.

0161.name: Fire Alarm
0161.description: Break glass, press here: a red that interrupts.

0162.name: Erythrosine
0162.description: Food colouring E127, the red of glacé cherries and dental disclosing tablets.

0163.name: Scarlet
0163.description: The red of cardinals' robes, letter-A infamy and military coats; dyed with kermes, then cochineal.

0164.name: Neon Cherry
0164.description: A cherry that swallowed the sign outside the bar.

0165.name: Impossible Red
0165.description: Redder than an sRGB screen can render; this one lives in the Rec2020 gamut's outer shell.

0166.name: Tomato
0166.description: Vine-ripe red with orange in its blood; botanically a fruit, culinarily an argument.

0167.name: Dusty Coral
0167.description: Coral that has spent a decade as a sofa cushion.

0168.name: Grapefruit
0168.description: The pink-red flesh of the breakfast half, sugar optional.

0169.name: Red Mana
0169.description: The colour you tap for direct damage: mountains, dragons, impulse decisions.

0170.name: Muted Clay
0170.description: Terracotta with the volume knob rolled back.

0171.name: Cinnabar
0171.description: Mercury sulphide, the toxic ore behind vermilion; beautiful, and historically hazardous to its miners.

0172.name: Lifebuoy
0172.description: The ring on the pier rail, orange-red kept bright so it can be found in a grey sea.

0173.name: Robot Heart
0173.description: The warning-lamp red that blinks where a heart would be: feelings, printed on a circuit board.

0174.name: Coral Pink
0174.description: The classic reef-coral pink of mid-century bathrooms and lipsticks.

0175.name: Nemo
0175.description: The clownfish who got found: orange-red between white stripes.

0176.name: Georgia Peach
0176.description: The state fruit at full blush, more red than a peach has any right to be.

0177.name: Living Coral
0177.description: Pantone's 2019 Colour of the Year, chosen as a plea for the reefs that were bleaching white.

0178.name: Blush Beige
0178.description: Foundation-counter neutral with a warm secret.

0179.name: Glowing Coral
0179.description: Coral polyps under a blacklight tide.

0180.name: Red Valerian
0180.description: The wall-loving wildflower that colonises cliffs and railway cuttings in coral-red drifts.

0181.name: Koi
0181.description: The orange-red flash under the pond surface; ornamental carp bred for centuries in Japan.

0182.name: Ceviche
0182.description: Citrus-cured fish blushing pink in lime juice, cooked by acid instead of fire.

0183.name: Salmon
0183.description: The fish so chromatically committed it named the colour; farmed ones have to eat their pink.

0184.name: Dusty Pink
0184.description: Pink through a lace curtain that needs washing.

0185.name: Pink Sherbet
0185.description: Scooped fizzy-sweet pink, paper cup included.

0186.name: Poke Bowl
0186.description: Cubed raw salmon over rice, Hawaiian lunch-counter pink-orange.

0188.name: Perfect Pink
0188.description: The pink you mix on the third try and refuse to touch again.

0189.name: Conch
0189.description: The shell's flared lip, pale coral-pink and ocean-polished; hold it up and hear the sea.

0190.name: Angel Wing
0190.description: Named for the delicate pink begonia, and the paired seashells that open like wings.

0191.name: Young Salmon
0191.description: A smolt-fresh salmon pink, still pale from the river.

0192.name: Dawn Chorus
0192.description: The colour of the sky when the first bird refuses to let you sleep.

0193.name: Ephemeral Red
0193.description: The palest tint in the red row, a red at the exact moment of vanishing.

0194.name: Black Bean
0194.description: Frijoles negros before the pot: black with a red-brown gleam.

0195.name: Dark Rust
0195.description: Iron oxide that has been at it for years.

0196.name: Dark Chocolate
0196.description: Eighty-five percent cacao: bitter enough to be called sophisticated.

0197.name: Henna
0197.description: The bridal dye: lawsone from crushed leaves, staining skin and hair red-brown for weeks.

0198.name: Red Ochre
0198.description: Iron-rich earth, humanity's first paint; the red of Lascaux and every ochre pit since.

0199.name: Burnt Brick
0199.description: A brick that went through the kiln twice.

0200.name: Rosy Taupe
0200.description: Mole-grey warmed by a rose standing nearby.

0201.name: Rust
0201.description: Iron oxide blooming where metal met water.

0202.name: Chilli
0202.description: Dried red chillies strung up to concentrate their threat.

0203.name: Brown
0203.description: The colour vision science calls low-luminance orange; context does all the work.

0205.name: Terracotta
0205.description: Literally 'baked earth': the pot, the roof tile, the warrior army at Xi'an.

0206.name: Clay
0206.description: Riverbank clay on the wheel, before the glaze gets a vote.

0207.name: Burnt Orange
0207.description: Orange pushed to the edge of brown, claimed by two rival universities and all of autumn.

0208.name: Blood Orange
0208.description: The winter citrus whose anthocyanin flush needs cold nights to develop.

0209.name: Paprika
0209.description: Ground sweet peppers, Hungary's national spice and arguably its national colour.

0210.name: Canyon Clay
0210.description: Southwestern canyon walls an hour before sunset.

0211.name: Cayenne
0211.description: The pepper powder measured in pinches and regretted in tablespoons.

0212.name: Persimmon
0212.description: The autumn fruit that hangs on after the leaves drop, orange-red lanterns on bare branches.

0213.name: Vermilion
0213.description: Ground cinnabar: the sacred red of Chinese lacquer, Roman frescoes and temple gates.

0214.name: Flame
0214.description: The orange middle of the fire, between blue heart and yellow tip.

0215.name: Rocket Red
0215.description: Launch-pad red-orange, felt in the sternum.

0216.name: Cedar
0216.description: Freshly split cedar heartwood, aromatic and reddish.

0217.name: Red Orange
0217.description: The crayon that couldn't decide and was right not to.

0218.name: Rosy Tan
0218.description: Tan warmed by a rosy undertone.

0219.name: Adobe
0219.description: Sun-dried mud brick, the warm buff-orange of pueblo walls. The software came later.

0220.name: Orange You Glad
0220.description: The knock-knock punchline in pigment form; banana jokes not included.

0221.name: Safety Orange
0221.description: The regulation hi-vis orange of cones, vests and hunting caps, chosen to clash with everything in nature.

0222.name: Realgar
0222.description: Arsenic sulphide, the 'ruby of arsenic': a gorgeous orange-red mineral you should not lick.

0223.name: Nasturtium
0223.description: The trailing garden flower you can eat: peppery leaves, flame-orange petals.

0224.name: Coral
0224.description: The reef-builder's skeleton in its famous orange-pink; the colour keeps the name even as reefs lose it.

0225.name: Tangerine
0225.description: The easy-peel citrus, named for Tangier; orange with the brightness up.

0226.name: Mushroom
0226.description: Field-mushroom cap: greige with an earthy pink underside.

0227.name: Chanterelle
0227.description: The apricot-scented golden mushroom foragers won't tell you the location of.

0228.name: Minium
0228.description: Red lead, the pigment of mediaeval manuscript rubrics, whence 'miniature'.

0229.name: Fire Coral
0229.description: The stinging reef organism that is not quite coral and not quite polite.

0230.name: Cantaloupe
0230.description: Melon-ball orange, scooped on a hot day.

0231.name: Tiger of Mysore
0231.description: Tipu Sultan, who faced the East India Company with rockets and a mechanical tiger; a burning orange remembered.

0232.name: Gorgonian
0232.description: The sea fan, a coral relative spreading like orange lace in the current.

0234.name: Tabby
0234.description: The marmalade cat asleep in the one sunbeam.

0235.name: Bisque
0235.description: Lobster-shell soup and unglazed porcelain share this warm pale orange; both are called bisque.

0236.name: Highland Cow
0236.description: The rusty-orange fringe of Scotland's shaggiest export, peering through its own hair.

0237.name: Nectarine
0237.description: The smooth-skinned peach variety, all the blush and none of the fuzz.

0238.name: Juicy Peach
0238.description: The one you eat over the sink.

0239.name: Discreet Orange
0239.description: Orange trying not to draw attention to itself and, being orange, failing gracefully.

0240.name: Corgi
0240.description: The royal dog breed's orange-cream coat, short legs and all.

0241.name: First Light
0241.description: The sky's first warm answer to the night shift.

0242.name: Madeleine
0242.description: The shell-shaped sponge cake that unlocked Proust's entire childhood, pale golden with a browned edge.

0243.name: Alpenglow
0243.description: The rose-orange light on mountain peaks after the sun has left the valley; a real optical phenomenon with a German name.

0244.name: Black Coffee
0244.description: No milk, no sugar, no negotiation.

0245.name: Bean There Done That
0245.description: Espresso-dark smugness; the pun forgives the caffeine dependency.

0246.name: Molasses
0246.description: The slow dark syrup left when sugar is done crystallising.

0247.name: Burnt Umber
0247.description: Umber earth from Umbria, roasted to deepen; on every palette since the Baroque.

0248.name: Cold Brew
0248.description: Coffee steeped patient and dark, served over conviction.

0249.name: Saddle Brown
0249.description: Tack-room leather, oiled and ridden; an X11 stalwart.

0250.name: Russet
0250.description: The rough reddish-brown of homespun cloth and heritage apples, mediaeval England's working colour.

0251.name: Coffee
0251.description: With a splash of milk, if the pot is honest.

0252.name: Cinnamon
0252.description: The inner bark of a Sri Lankan tree, rolled and ground into warm brown-red.

0253.name: Mocha
0253.description: Coffee met chocolate, named for a Yemeni port that shipped both directions of brown.

0254.name: Gingerbread
0254.description: The structural cookie, brown enough to be architecture.

0255.name: Churro
0255.description: Fried dough dragged through cinnamon sugar, fairground brown-gold with ridges.

0256.name: Toffee
0256.description: Butter and sugar taken exactly far enough.

0257.name: Fulvous
0257.description: The naturalist's word for tawny, used almost exclusively for ducks and the people who watch them.

0258.name: Caramel
0258.description: Sugar past its melting point, one shade before regret.

0259.name: Copper
0259.description: The one orange metal: pipes, pennies and pre-patina domes.

0260.name: Hazelnut
0260.description: The shell of the nut the dormouse is sleeping in.

0261.name: Marmalade
0261.description: Seville oranges boiled bitter-bright, spread on toast.

0262.name: Orange
0262.description: The fruit that named the colour; before oranges reached Europe, English simply said yellow-red.

0263.name: Butterscotch
0263.description: Brown sugar and butter, the hard candy in every grandparent's dish.

0264.name: Smashing Pumpkins
0264.description: The band and the Halloween aftermath, both this orange.

0265.name: Cadmium Orange
0265.description: The opaque powerhouse of the paint box: cadmium sulfoselenide, brilliant and slightly notorious.

0266.name: Papaya
0266.description: Tropical flesh orange with a scent that divides households.

0267.name: Kumquat
0267.description: The citrus you eat whole, peel and all: a mouthful of orange contradiction.

0268.name: Tangerine Tango
0268.description: Pantone's 2012 Colour of the Year, a reddened dance-floor orange.

0269.name: Clementine
0269.description: The Christmas-stocking citrus: seedless, easy-peeling, gone by Boxing Day.

0270.name: Butternut
0270.description: The squash halved to reveal its dense orange interior.

0272.name: Polly
0272.description: The parrot that wants a cracker — peach-feathered variety.

0273.name: Gentle Caress
0273.description: A peach tone applied at the pressure of a compliment.

0274.name: Dark Bronze
0274.description: Statue bronze in the museum's worst-lit corridor.

0275.name: Truffle
0275.description: The black diggable kind — dark earthy brown, priced like contraband.

0276.name: Bronzite
0276.description: A bronze-lustred pyroxene mineral that glints like the metal it is named for.

0277.name: Sunken Gold
0277.description: Doubloons under two centuries of seawater: gold that gave up its shine but not its value.

0278.name: Maple
0278.description: Syrup grade: amber, dark, very dark. This one is pancake-ready.

0279.name: Honey Bronze
0279.description: Bronze cut with honey; a medal for third place in summer.

0280.name: Sandalwood
0280.description: The fragrant carving wood whose scent lasts decades — warm grey-brown.

0281.name: Old Gold
0281.description: The heraldic gold of faded banners, gilding softened by age.

0282.name: Deep Saffron
0282.description: Saffron threads packed dense, the deep orange of the spice at quantity.

0283.name: Camel
0283.description: The coat, not the animal, though the animal supplied the reference.

0284.name: Orpiment
0284.description: Golden arsenic sulphide; mediaeval illuminators risked their health for this yellow-orange.

0285.name: Harvest Gold
0285.description: The avocado-adjacent appliance colour of 1970s kitchens, unrepentant.

0286.name: Honey
0286.description: Runny, backlit on the breakfast table.

0287.name: Marigold
0287.description: The cempasúchil of Día de los Muertos, petals bright enough to guide the departed home.

0288.name: Tan
0288.description: From tannin-cured leather: the colour of the tanner's trade.

0290.name: Golden Sand
0290.description: The beach in the brochure, warm underfoot.

0291.name: Saffron
0291.description: The world's most expensive spice by weight: three crocus stigmas at a time.

0292.name: Warm Sand
0292.description: The beach at four in the afternoon, holding the day's heat.

0293.name: Burning Flame
0293.description: The bright middle of the campfire, seen through squinted eyes.

0294.name: Golden Hour
0294.description: The photographer's favourite sixty minutes, when everything is briefly flattering.

0295.name: Golden Straw
0295.description: The bale at the end of harvest, catching low sun.

0296.name: Aureolin
0296.description: Cobalt yellow, a transparent painter's yellow with a saint's-halo name.

0297.name: Wheat
0297.description: The field a week before harvest; an X11 classic.

0298.name: Golden Halo
0298.description: The gilded ring in every icon painting: sainthood as a colour.

0299.name: Buttercream
0299.description: Icing whipped with enough butter to blur the line between frosting and dessert.

0300.name: Cut the Mustard
0300.description: To be good enough; nobody agrees why mustard, but this dark spicy brown-gold qualifies.

0301.name: Bivouac
0301.description: Camp without tents: a groundsheet, a dark olive night, and morale.

0302.name: Antique Brass
0302.description: The doorknob a century of hands has polished dark.

0303.name: Coyote
0303.description: Desert-dog tan; also the military's official name for this exact shade of gear.

0304.name: Dark Brass
0304.description: The band instrument that skipped a few polishings.

0305.name: Dijon
0305.description: The Burgundian mustard city, sharper and browner than the ballpark stuff.

0306.name: Dusty Khaki
0306.description: Khaki after the march, which is arguably the only authentic khaki.

0307.name: Dukkah
0307.description: The Egyptian nut-and-spice blend you dip bread into via olive oil, toasty gold-brown.

0308.name: Weld
0308.description: The oldest European yellow dye plant; mediaeval dyers crossed it with woad to make green.

0309.name: Curry
0309.description: The powder, not any particular curry: turmeric-led and generously gold.

0310.name: Ochre
0310.description: Iron-tinted earth, humanity's longest-serving pigment; this is the golden middle of its range.

0311.name: Gamboge
0311.description: A saffron-yellow resin tapped from Cambodian trees; its name and Cambodia's share a root.

0312.name: Khaki
0312.description: Urdu for dust, adopted by armies when they noticed dust made better camouflage than scarlet.

0313.name: Honey Gold
0313.description: Honey held up to the window.

0314.name: Hay
0314.description: The meadow, cut and dried and stacked — summer stored as colour.

0315.name: Goldenrod
0315.description: The roadside wildflower that yellows entire late-summer fields; a crayon-box regular.

0316.name: Deadly Mustard
0316.description: Mustard with intent, a condiment gone rogue.

0317.name: Sandcastle
0317.description: Bucket-moulded architecture on a deadline set by the tide.

0318.name: Yell for Yellow
0318.description: Yellow at the volume where a cheer becomes a chant.

0319.name: Mustard
0319.description: The condiment that named the colour: ground seed, vinegar, and confidence.

0320.name: Sand
0320.description: Dune-coloured quartz, ground down by a patient ocean.

0321.name: Buddha's Love Handles
0321.description: The laughing Budai's generous golden belly, rubbed for luck, dressed in this amber.

0322.name: Sunflower
0322.description: The flower that tracks the sun all day and then, mature, faces east forever.

0323.name: Midas
0323.description: The king whose touch turned everything gold, a blessing for exactly one day.

0324.name: Champagne
0324.description: The pale gold in the flute; technically only from Champagne, colloquially from anywhere celebratory.

0325.name: Golden Cream
0325.description: Cream skimmed from milk that grazed in buttercups.

0326.name: Golden Aura
0326.description: The glow around someone who just got good news.

0327.name: Candlelight
0327.description: The warm radius of a single flame, the original mood lighting.

0328.name: Sunrise Cream
0328.description: The sky's first soft gold, whipped.

0329.name: Peat
0329.description: Compressed bog-plant millennia: the near-black brown that flavours whisky.

0330.name: Dark Olive
0330.description: The olive at the bottom of the jar, steeped darkest.

0331.name: Olive Drab
0331.description: The exact regulation green-brown of mid-century armies, drab by name and by order.

0332.name: Olive
0332.description: The Mediterranean fruit whose green-brown ambiguity named a whole colour family.

0333.name: Fustic
0333.description: A yellow dyewood shipped from the Caribbean to colour Europe's cloth for centuries.

0334.name: Miso
0334.description: Fermented soybean paste; the darker and older, the deeper this gold-brown gets.

0335.name: Tapenade
0335.description: Olives crushed with capers into a spreadable dark gold-green.

0336.name: Natterjack
0336.description: Britain's loudest toad, olive-backed with a racing stripe.

0337.name: Lost in Time
0337.description: The colour of a photograph left in a drawer since a decade nobody labels.

0338.name: Citron
0338.description: The knobbly ancestral citrus, more peel than fruit, all of it this yellow-gold.

0339.name: Matcha
0339.description: Stone-ground shade-grown tea, whisked; green with a golden undertone.

0340.name: Piccalilli
0340.description: The lurid British mustard pickle — a relish that glows in the jar.

0341.name: Olive or Twist
0341.description: The Dickensian question every martini answers: olive-gold either way, please sir.

0342.name: Split Pea
0342.description: The soup legume, dried and halved: a green-gold that thickens on standing.

0343.name: Lemongrass
0343.description: The citrus-scented stalk of Thai kitchens, pale gold-green when bruised.

0344.name: Straw
0344.description: What's left when the grain is gone: pale gold you can weave.

0345.name: Gold
0345.description: The metal that never tarnishes; this is its colour as pigment rather than shine.

0346.name: Flax
0346.description: The plant behind linen and linseed; its dried stalks are exactly this blond.

0347.name: Parchment
0347.description: Prepared skin, scraped thin, holding a thousand years of handwriting.

0348.name: Maize
0348.description: Field corn gold, the Americas' founding grain.

0349.name: Cadmium Yellow
0349.description: The opaque workhorse yellow of oil painting; Monet insisted on it.

0350.name: Vanilla
0350.description: The orchid pod's flavour, synonymous with plain, which is unfair to a spice this expensive.

0351.name: Dandelion
0351.description: The lawn's stubborn yellow, a thousand small suns going to seed.

0352.name: Sunburst
0352.description: The guitar finish and the actual event, both radiating gold.

0353.name: Eggnog
0353.description: Cream, sugar and egg: the holiday drink in its pale gold glass.

0354.name: Spätzle Yellow
0354.description: Swabian egg noodles scraped fresh into the pot, comfort in pale yellow.

0355.name: Cosmic Latte
0355.description: The measured average colour of the universe. Astronomers surveyed 200,000 galaxies and got a milky beige; this point is the nearest neighbour to the official #fff8e7.

0356.name: Bollywood Gold
0356.description: The shade of every wedding-scene sari and its two hundred sequinned backup dancers.

0357.name: Moonbeam
0357.description: Moonlight is sunlight, reflected and softened: pale gold, gently delivered.

0358.name: Forest Floor
0358.description: Leaf litter, moss shadow and things that prefer not to be seen: a green-black compost dark.

0359.name: Army Green
0359.description: The issue colour of fatigues and surplus stores everywhere.

0360.name: Thyme
0360.description: The low herb that survives drought, frost and being stepped on; grey-green and stoic.

0361.name: Foxhole
0361.description: Dug-in dark olive; you and the earth, keeping each other's counsel.

0362.name: Verdant Bronze
0362.description: Bronze going green, the moment before patina fully wins.

0363.name: Dill Pickle
0363.description: The barrel-fermented deli green, briny and dependable.

0364.name: Camouflage
0364.description: The pattern designed to make colour disappear; this is its most common single swatch.

0365.name: Moss
0365.description: The soft green that grows where nothing else volunteers: north sides of trees, hearts of shade.

0366.name: Willow
0366.description: Willow leaves silvering in wind, grey-green with a riverbank address.

0367.name: Wasabi
0367.description: The real rhizome is this muted olive-green; the neon paste at most sushi bars is dyed horseradish.

0368.name: Lichen
0368.description: A fungus and an alga in a very long marriage, painting rocks grey-green.

0369.name: Green Olive
0369.description: The martini garnish, picked young and brined bright.

0371.name: Kiwi
0371.description: The fuzzy fruit's bright interior, black seeds in green flesh; named for a brown flightless bird, confusingly.

0372.name: Warbler
0372.description: Olive-yellow and heard more than seen; the birder's favourite identification headache.

0373.name: Dirty Martini
0373.description: Gin clouded with olive brine, cocktail hour's murkiest green-gold.

0374.name: Celery
0374.description: The pale green stalk, mostly water and string.

0375.name: Hops
0375.description: The bitter green cones that made beer worth arguing about.

0376.name: Absinthe
0376.description: The green fairy of Belle Époque Paris: wormwood, anise, and a century-long ban.

0377.name: Sweet Pea
0377.description: The climbing flower bred for scent, in the gentlest green-adjacent pastel of the trellis.

0378.name: High-Vis
0378.description: Regulation fluorescent yellow-green: the colour of being legally impossible to ignore.

0379.name: Pear
0379.description: Anjou skin green-gold, ripe the moment you stop checking.

0380.name: Winter Pear
0380.description: The late-harvest varieties that sweeten in cold storage while nobody watches.

0381.name: Pickleball
0381.description: The perforated plastic ball of America's fastest-growing racquet sport, optic yellow-green.

0382.name: Beige and Sage
0382.description: Beige and sage, the rhyming duo of calm interiors, meeting in one swatch.

0383.name: Chartreuse Yellow
0383.description: The yellow half of the liqueur's famous ambiguity.

0384.name: Limoncello
0384.description: Amalfi lemon peel steeped in spirit, served freezing and glowing faintly.

0385.name: Lime Zest
0385.description: The oily green shavings that carry all the perfume.

0386.name: Canary
0386.description: The cage-bird bred yellow from a greenish wild finch; named for the islands it came from.

0387.name: Tennis Ball
0387.description: Optic yellow, adopted in 1972 so television viewers could follow the serve.

0388.name: Key Lime
0388.description: The tiny Florida lime of pie fame, sharper and yellower than its supermarket cousin.

0389.name: Elderflower
0389.description: The hedgerow blossom pressed into cordial, pale green-gold with a June scent.

0390.name: Appletini
0390.description: The 1990s' most chemically green cocktail, served with irony optional.

0391.name: Electric Lime
0391.description: A Crayola-era fluorescent, lime at mains voltage.

0392.name: Winter Melon
0392.description: The wax gourd of Cantonese soups, frosted pale green and big as a toddler.

0393.name: Lemon Posset
0393.description: The three-ingredient British dessert: cream, sugar, lemon; the palest yellow set.

0394.name: Laser Lemon
0394.description: Crayola's fluorescent lemon, the crayon that seemed to emit rather than reflect.

0395.name: Antifreeze
0395.description: Ethylene glycol's warning-bright green, dyed this colour precisely so you notice the leak.

0396.name: Lemon
0396.description: The citrus that set the standard for yellow.

0397.name: Crème de la Crème
0397.description: The cream of the cream — the very best, skimmed twice, appropriately pale and rich.

0398.name: Honeysuckle
0398.description: The hedge climber children learn to sip, one drop of nectar per pale yellow trumpet.

0399.name: Yellow
0399.description: The brightest primary in most people's heads: bee stripes, warning signs, and the sun as drawn by every child.

0400.name: Safety Vest
0400.description: Fluorescent yellow with reflective stripes, the uniform of being seen.

0401.name: Glowworm
0401.description: A beetle, not a worm, running its own pale green lantern on bioluminescence.

0402.name: Rifle Green
0402.description: The near-black green of 19th-century rifle regiments, early camouflage born from Sherwood-style scouting.

0403.name: Dark Fern
0403.description: Fern fronds in the deepest part of the understorey.

0404.name: Hunter Green
0404.description: The traditional deep green of hunting dress, and of every prep-school blazer since.

0405.name: Sap Green
0405.description: Historically made from buckthorn berries; the watercolourist's transparent leafy green.

0406.name: Fern
0406.description: The unfurling frond, a green older than flowers.

0407.name: Sagebrush
0407.description: The silvery scrub of the American West, smelling of rain that rarely comes.

0408.name: Nettle
0408.description: The sting is formic acid; the green is chlorophyll; the soup is surprisingly good.

0409.name: Bracken
0409.description: The fern that owns entire hillsides, green in June, copper by October.

0410.name: Grasshopper
0410.description: The meadow jumper tuned to match its lawn.

0411.name: Avocado
0411.description: The fruit, the toast, the 1970s bathroom suite: one green, three destinies.

0412.name: Grass Green
0412.description: Lawn at regulation height, the green the word green usually means.

0413.name: Cactus
0413.description: Waxy desert green, built to store water behind spines.

0414.name: Radium Dial
0414.description: The self-luminous watch paint of the 1920s, a glow with a tragic factory history.

0415.name: Asparagus
0415.description: The spring spear, green from tip to snap point.

0416.name: Venom Green
0416.description: Nature's warning label: green that advises against touching.

0417.name: Eucalyptus
0417.description: The koala's entire menu: silvery blue-green leaves full of menthol oil.

0418.name: Guac and Roll
0418.description: Avocado smashed with attitude, the tableside anthem.

0419.name: Apple Green
0419.description: The classic bright green of orchard apples, a colour name since the 1600s.

0420.name: Pistachio
0420.description: The nut whose green means quality; Sicilian growers guard it like a vintage.

0421.name: Sour Apple
0421.description: The candy flavour that puckers, greener than any real apple dares.

0422.name: Holy Guacamole
0422.description: The exclamation and the dip: avocado green elevated to minor miracle.

0424.name: Toxic Green
0424.description: The barrel-with-a-skull-label green of every cartoon hazard.

0425.name: Celadon
0425.description: The jade-toned Asian glaze a millennium of potters chased; named, oddly, after a French stage shepherd.

0427.name: Snap Pea
0427.description: The pod you eat whole: sweet, crisp, audibly green.

0428.name: Highlighter Green
0428.description: The fluorescent marker for text you'll pretend to revisit.

0429.name: Fiddle-Leaf Fig
0429.description: The houseplant influencers made famous: glossy violin-shaped leaves, dramatic about draughts.

0430.name: Dusty Miller
0430.description: The silver-leaved bedding plant, grey-green foliage that reads as moonlight.

0431.name: Caterpillar
0431.description: The leaf-eating machine matching its food, green until the butterfly.

0432.name: Green Grape
0432.description: The seedless supermarket bunch, pale green and refrigerated.

0433.name: Lime
0433.description: The citrus that named a green, sharper than lemon in every sense.

0434.name: Firefly
0434.description: Cold light from a summer beetle: luciferin green, blinking in code.

0435.name: Tea Green
0435.description: Steeped green-tea leaves, soft and yellowed.

0436.name: Spring Bud
0436.description: The first swelling on the branch, a classic name for the year's newest green.

0437.name: Sour Green
0437.description: The candy-aisle green that makes your jaw ache preemptively.

0438.name: Limelight
0438.description: Theatres once spotlit stages by heating lime until it glowed; hence the fame idiom and this green-white.

0439.name: Celadon Cream
0439.description: The famous glaze whipped into something softer.

0440.name: Lime Rickey
0440.description: The soda-fountain classic: lime, fizz, and a colour to match.

0441.name: Honeydew
0441.description: The pale green melon, refreshing in fact and in name.

0442.name: Katydid
0442.description: The leaf-mimic insect that sings its own name all night.

0443.name: Chartreuse
0443.description: The liqueur 130 herbs and two Carthusian monks keep secret; the only colour named after a drink recipe.

0444.name: Pistachio Cream
0444.description: Sicilian pistachio paste folded into sweetness, gelato's palest green.

0445.name: Gimlet
0445.description: Gin and lime cordial, sharp as the tool it's named after.

0446.name: Green Juice
0446.description: Kale, cucumber, apple and optimism, blended.

0447.name: Springwater
0447.description: Cold from the rock, faintly green over stones.

0448.name: Bear Den
0448.description: The dark under the roots where winter is slept through.

0449.name: Phthalo Green
0449.description: The staining modern pigment so intense a fingertip of it tints a whole wash.

0450.name: Juniper
0450.description: The berry that makes gin gin: dusty blue on a spiky evergreen.

0451.name: British Racing Green
0451.description: The national motorsport colour Britain adopted after racing in Ireland; Bentley's dark green.

0452.name: Forest Green
0452.description: The canopy from underneath, the standard dark green of maps and waxed jackets.

0453.name: Evergreen
0453.description: The trees that skip autumn entirely; also anything that never goes out of date.

0454.name: Old Growth
0454.description: Forest that has never met a saw: moss on moss, centuries deep.

0455.name: Dark Moss
0455.description: Moss in the crevice the sun never reaches.

0456.name: Deep Malachite
0456.description: The banded copper mineral at its darkest ring.

0457.name: Rosemary
0457.description: The remembrance herb, needles of dusty green that survive on neglect.

0458.name: Ivy
0458.description: The climber that outlives the wall, evergreen and tenacious.

0459.name: Pine Green
0459.description: The needle green of conifers, standard issue for forests and bottle labels.

0460.name: Chlorophyll
0460.description: The molecule that eats sunlight, the reason green is Earth's default from orbit.

0461.name: Hedge
0461.description: The suburban property line, trimmed to attention.

0462.name: Lawn
0462.description: Grass under contract: mown, edged, and argued about with neighbours.

0463.name: Mossy Ruins
0463.description: Stonework losing an argument with moss, beautifully.

0464.name: Kelly Green
0464.description: The bright Irish-American green of parades and jerseys; named after the surname, not a person in particular.

0465.name: Smaragdine
0465.description: An archaic word for emerald-green, the 1935 spelling-bee word that made headlines.

0466.name: Verdure
0466.description: The old word for lush growing greenness itself.

0467.name: Turf
0467.description: Grass you can buy in rolls; territory you defend without paperwork.

0468.name: Malachite
0468.description: The swirling banded copper carbonate; Russian tsars panelled entire rooms in it.

0469.name: Grass
0469.description: The plain green underfoot, the most stepped-on colour in the world.

0470.name: Wimbledon
0470.description: Centre Court in week one, before the baselines wear to dust.

0471.name: Meadow
0471.description: Unmown grass with flowers in it, a lawn that got away.

0472.name: Clover
0472.description: Three leaves for luck's baseline, four for the jackpot.

0473.name: Shamrock
0473.description: The three-leafed sprig St Patrick allegedly taught with: Ireland in lapel form.

0474.name: Pestering Pesto
0474.description: Basil sauce that gets on everything, persistently green.

0475.name: Marimo
0475.description: The velvet algae balls of Lake Akan, kept as slow-motion pets in jars.

0476.name: Greenlight
0476.description: Permission as a colour: the project is go.

0477.name: Romaine
0477.description: The Caesar-salad lettuce, crisp-ribbed and upright.

0478.name: Dioptase
0478.description: The emerald-green copper mineral that fooled prospectors hunting actual emeralds.

0479.name: Watercress
0479.description: The peppery stream-grown green of proper sandwiches.

0480.name: Mangetout
0480.description: French for 'eat everything': the pea pod that skips shelling.

0481.name: Parakeet
0481.description: Small parrot, maximum green: the escaped-pet flocks of every warm city.

0482.name: Green
0482.description: The colour human eyes distinguish best; we evolved in it.

0483.name: Vine Snake
0483.description: The whip-thin snake that impersonates a vine until something comes close.

0484.name: Tree Python
0484.description: The emerald tree python, coiled over a branch like a green question mark.

0486.name: Tadpole
0486.description: A frog in progress, pond-green with a deadline.

0488.name: Nopal
0488.description: The prickly-pear paddle, grilled and served — cactus as vegetable.

0489.name: Putting Green
0489.description: The most pampered grass in sport, mown to millimetres.

0490.name: Alien Green
0490.description: The regulation colour of flying-saucer occupants since pulp covers began.

0491.name: Aurora Green
0491.description: The oxygen-line green of the northern lights, the aurora's most common colour.

0492.name: Quetzal
0492.description: The resplendent Mesoamerican bird whose tail feathers were currency; too iridescent for one hex.

0493.name: Light Moss
0493.description: Moss in the one sunny spot.

0494.name: Leprechaun
0494.description: The gold-hoarding cobbler of Irish folklore, dressed head to buckle in this green.

0495.name: Fern Green
0495.description: The mid-green of a frond at full unfurl.

0496.name: Battletoad
0496.description: The bruiser amphibians of the notoriously difficult 1991 game, speeder-bike level green.

0497.name: Slime
0497.description: The ectoplasmic goo of games and cartoons: viscous, green, oddly beloved.

0498.name: Radioactive Green
0498.description: How movies think radiation looks; actual radiation is invisible, which tested poorly.

0499.name: Neon Green
0499.description: Actual neon glows red-orange — this green borrowed the name and kept the wattage.

0500.name: Grass Stain
0500.description: The knee of every childhood trouser, chlorophyll's signature.

0501.name: Meadow Mist
0501.description: The field at dawn, wearing weather.

0502.name: Menthol
0502.description: The cooling molecule from mint, green that tastes cold.

0503.name: Leaf Bud
0503.description: The week the twig decides on green.

0504.name: Wheatgrass
0504.description: The juice-bar shot of concentrated lawn.

0505.name: Spring Green
0505.description: The season's official colour, a web-standard green between green and cyan.

0506.name: Gumdrop Green
0506.description: The sugared dome nobody picks first from the box; lime flavoured, allegedly.

0507.name: Splat
0507.description: The cartoon sound effect, rendered in slime green.

0508.name: Out of Left Field
0508.description: Baseball's deep grass, where the unexpected comes from.

0509.name: Butterhead
0509.description: The soft lettuce with the flattering name — pale loose leaves, butter-tender.

0510.name: Budgie Green
0510.description: The classic wild budgerigar colourway before breeders discovered blue.

0511.name: Jolly Green
0511.description: The giant of tinned-vegetable fame, ho-ho-ho included.

0512.name: Impossible Green
0512.description: A green beyond sRGB's reach; you need a wide-gamut display to see what it actually is.

0513.name: Gamut Green
0513.description: Named for the boundary it lives on, the edge of what your screen can show.

0514.name: Frosted Fir
0514.description: The tree lot in December, pre-dusted.

0515.name: Lime Green
0515.description: The citrus green of every flavour promising refreshment.

0517.name: Northern Lights
0517.description: The aurora borealis in its dominant oxygen green, curtaining a polar sky.

0518.name: Mantis
0518.description: The praying predator that is 90% camouflage, 10% patience.

0519.name: Aphid
0519.description: The pale green greenfly that farms your roses before ladybirds farm it.

0520.name: Laser Pointer
0520.description: The 532-nanometre green dot, brighter to human eyes than red and irresistible to cats.

0521.name: Nuclear Green
0521.description: Pop culture's glow-in-the-dark reactor green; see also every ooze barrel since 1984.

0522.name: Mojito
0522.description: Muddled mint, lime, rum and ice: Havana's greenest export.

0524.name: Lightning Bug
0524.description: The American firefly: same cold green light, friendlier name.

0525.name: Green Screen
0525.description: Chroma-key green, chosen because human skin contains none of it; the colour of everything replaceable.

0526.name: Dart Frog
0526.description: Tiny, brilliant, and toxic in proportion to its colour confidence.

0527.name: Waiting Room
0527.description: Institutional pale mint, chosen decades ago to be calming.

0528.name: Cucumber
0528.description: Cool by reputation and by measurement, the salad's pale green.

0529.name: Creamy Mint
0529.description: Mint choc chip minus the chips.

0530.name: Morning Dew
0530.description: The lawn's overnight jewellery, gone by nine.

0531.name: Will-o'-Wisp
0531.description: The ghost-light of marshes that led travellers astray: probably swamp gas, definitely this pale green.

0532.name: Void Green
0532.description: Green at the edge of black, the last green before nothing.

0533.name: Dark Pine
0533.description: The plantation interior at noon, which is to say dusk.

0534.name: Hooker's Green
0534.description: Named for botanical illustrator William Hooker, who mixed it for painting leaves.

0535.name: Bottle Green
0535.description: Victorian glass-bottle green: dark, dignified, school-uniform approved.

0536.name: Understory
0536.description: The forest layer that lives on leftover light.

0537.name: Brunswick Green
0537.description: The deep green of German coach paint and British railway engines.

0538.name: Hemlock
0538.description: The shade-loving conifer; also, botanically unrelated, the poison Socrates drank.

0539.name: Deep Pond
0539.description: Where the bottom stops being visible and starts being imagined.

0540.name: Sacramento Green
0540.description: The dark state-capital green of California's flag field and vintage Cadillacs.

0541.name: Dark Ivy
0541.description: Ivy on the north wall, in its winter coat.

0542.name: Cadmium Green
0542.description: Cadmium yellow blended with viridian — the painter's shortcut to sunlit leaves.

0543.name: Moor-Monster
0543.description: Whatever makes that sound out on the bog after dark, presumed green.

0544.name: Amazon
0544.description: The river-and-rainforest green visible from space, the planet's largest single statement in this colour.

0545.name: Ficus Elastica
0545.description: The rubber plant: glossy deep leaves that tolerate your living room's worst corner.

0546.name: Viridian
0546.description: The blue-leaning chromium green on every painter's palette since the 1840s.

0547.name: Rainforest
0547.description: Canopy on canopy, green with 100% humidity.

0548.name: Uvarovite
0548.description: The rarest garnet, permanently emerald-green thanks to chromium; usually too small to cut.

0549.name: Emerald Rain
0549.description: A jewel-green downpour, the forest replenishing its own colour.

0550.name: Croupier
0550.description: The card-table baize from the dealer's side.

0551.name: Billiard Green
0551.description: Regulation table baize — wool cloth in the exact green of misspent evenings.

0552.name: Deep Jungle
0552.description: Layers past where the tourists turn back.

0553.name: Elephant Ear
0553.description: The giant taro leaf, big enough to umbrella under.

0554.name: Emerald City
0554.description: Oz's capital, green by decree, and by the tinted glasses everyone was made to wear.

0555.name: Beryl
0555.description: The mineral family that contains emerald and aquamarine; this is its green heartland.

0556.name: Myrtle
0556.description: The evergreen shrub of Venus and wedding bouquets, a deep classical green.

0557.name: Pounamu
0557.description: New Zealand greenstone, treasured jade carved and passed down in Māori tradition.

0558.name: Serpentine
0558.description: The snake-skin green mineral that polishes like scales.

0559.name: Irish Green
0559.description: The forty shades condensed to one, flag-stripe green.

0560.name: Casino Green
0560.description: The felt where probability quietly wins.

0561.name: Later Gator
0561.description: The swamp farewell — see you in a while, crocodile.

0562.name: Monster Monstera
0562.description: The Swiss-cheese plant grown past its trellis and its welcome.

0563.name: Lucky Green
0563.description: The green you wear to the casino against all statistical advice.

0564.name: Signal Green
0564.description: The go-light green of railway and traffic signals, standardised permission.

0565.name: Emerald
0565.description: Beryl with chromium: the gem whose flaws are called a garden and forgiven.

0566.name: Beanpole
0566.description: The garden cane wearing runner beans by August.

0567.name: Chrome Green
0567.description: The industrial chromium-oxide green of machinery and money printing.

0568.name: Green Revolution
0568.description: The mid-century agricultural transformation that doubled harvests, wheat-field green at scale.

0569.name: Peacock Green
0569.description: The green flash in the eyespot of a peacock's train.

0570.name: Malachite Green
0570.description: The banded mineral's colour as a dye; also, controversially, an aquarium fungicide.

0571.name: Kashmir
0571.description: The valley's terraced green between the Himalayas, the colour behind all that wool.

0573.name: Shamrock Green
0573.description: The brighter, flag-adjacent cousin of the sprig itself.

0574.name: Fennel Fiasco
0574.description: When the aniseed herb takes over the entire allotment — a feathery green coup.

0575.name: Prairie
0575.description: Grassland to the horizon in every direction, green going on forever.

0576.name: Kryptonite
0576.description: The glowing green mineral that humbles Superman, weakness as a colour.

0578.name: Jade
0578.description: The stone of emperors and heirlooms: nephrite and jadeite sharing one revered green.

0579.name: Cicada
0579.description: The green-bodied summer noisemaker that spends seventeen years rehearsing underground.

0580.name: Veronese Green
0580.description: Named for the Venetian painter whose canvases run on this green; see his Wedding at Cana.

0581.name: Tobago
0581.description: The quieter half of Trinidad and Tobago, rainforest ridge meeting reef green.

0582.name: Secret Garden
0582.description: Behind the locked door in the ivy, green kept private.

0583.name: Intoxicate
0583.description: The green of the drink you should have refused.

0584.name: Bok Choy
0584.description: The spoon-shaped Chinese cabbage: white stems, green leaves.

0585.name: Jadeite
0585.description: The rarer, glassier jade, imperial green at auction-house prices.

0586.name: Verdelite
0586.description: Green tourmaline by its trade name, the 'little green one'.

0587.name: Maximum Green
0587.description: The most green a green can green.

0588.name: Catnip
0588.description: The grey-green herb that turns dignified cats into liquid chaos.

0589.name: Biolume Green
0589.description: The glow of living light: plankton, fungi and fish that make their own.

0590.name: Turtle Warrior
0590.description: A shelled fighter in ninja green, cowabunga adjacent.

0591.name: Frog Pond
0591.description: Duckweed-skinned water with eyes just above the surface.

0592.name: Alienated
0592.description: Green with existential distance; the UFO left without it.

0593.name: Spirogyra
0593.description: The spiral-chloroplast algae of biology class, pond scum with a beautiful secret under the microscope.

0594.name: Magical Malachite
0594.description: The banded green stone with folklore duties, protection mostly.

0595.name: Verde
0595.description: Green in Spanish and Italian alike.

0596.name: Matcha Foam
0596.description: The whisked crown of properly made matcha — jade froth.

0597.name: Bermudagrass
0597.description: The warm-climate turf of golf courses and lawns that survive August.

0598.name: Tsavorite

0599.name: Luciferin
0599.description: The light-bearing molecule behind every firefly flash and glowing wave.

0601.name: Celery Soda
0601.description: The deli counter's strangest survivor: celery-flavoured fizz, faithfully pale green.

0602.name: Seaweed
0602.description: The tide's green laundry, draped on every rock.

0603.name: Green Tourmaline
0603.description: The gem that comes in every colour but wears green best.

0604.name: Gaspeite
0604.description: A rare apple-green nickel mineral from Quebec's Gaspé Peninsula.

0605.name: Springtide

0606.name: Terrarium
0606.description: A garden under glass, running its own weather.

0607.name: Foxfire

0608.name: Demantoid

0612.name: Limonana

0614.name: Mint Condition

0615.name: Springtail
0615.description: The tiny leaping soil arthropod that keeps the pun and the garden.

0616.name: Aquamint

0617.name: Toothpaste

0619.name: Prasiolite

0621.name: Conservatory

0622.name: Quantum Green

0623.name: Vernal Green

0624.name: Parrotfish
0624.description: The reef grazer that crunches coral and, ahem, manufactures beaches.

0625.name: Chroma Green

0627.name: Grotesque Green

0628.name: Spirulina
0628.description: The blue-green algae of smoothie bowls and space-agency menus.

0631.name: Snow Pea

0632.name: Winterfresh

0633.name: Spearmint
0633.description: The milder mint, chewing gum's house flavour.

0634.name: Seafoam

0635.name: Bay
0635.description: The sheltered water between headlands, pale green over sand.

0638.name: Mint
0638.description: The herb that made fresh a colour; toothpaste, gum and mojitos all borrow this green.

0639.name: Illicit Green
0639.description: A green so vivid it feels like it should require a licence.

0640.name: Shallows
0640.description: Where the sea is still practising being land, pale green over sand.

0641.name: Backlit Mint

0642.name: Polo Mint
0642.description: The mint with the hole, Britain's circular breath freshener since 1948.

0643.name: Mint Cream

0644.name: Frost Flower

0645.name: Kelp Forest
0645.description: Underwater redwoods of algae, swaying at whale height.

0646.name: Phthalo Teal

0648.name: Deep Spruce

0649.name: Everglade

0650.name: Blue Hole

0651.name: Philodendron

0652.name: Paludarium

0653.name: Seagrass

0654.name: Green Slate

0656.name: Tropical Rainforest
0656.description: The biome with more shades of green than names for them.

0657.name: Pine

0658.name: Elf
0658.description: Workshop-issue green, pointy hat sold separately.

0659.name: Patina

0661.name: Go Go Green

0663.name: Amazonite

0679.name: Ninja Turtle

0681.name: Paddleboard

0682.name: Aventurine

0683.name: Glowing Lagoon

0687.name: Aloe

0691.name: Vernal Pool

0692.name: Calamansi

0693.name: Pristine Oceanic

0694.name: Mouthwash

0695.name: Cool Mint

0698.name: Island Mint

0750.description: Palawan's lagoon town, limestone cliffs around water this exact colour.

0752.description: The cruise ship's pool level; towel wars begin at dawn.

0756.name: Glitch

0767.name: Aloe Water
0767.description: The spa-fridge drink with the floating cubes of succulent.

0775.description: The ship-taking cephalopod of Norse legend, the dark under the dark.

0776.name: Petrol

0777.name: Nautilus

0778.name: Tidal Teal

0786.name: Spark Plug

0794.name: Baltic
0794.description: The brackish northern sea, cold teal between nine countries.

0795.name: Luminous Lagoon

0796.name: Copper Dome
0796.description: The cathedral roof a century into its green-teal patina.

0797.name: Pacific Blue
0797.description: The crayon named after the calmest-sounding ocean.

0798.name: Dusty Aqua
0798.description: Aqua found at the back of the cupboard.

0799.name: Iznik

0801.name: Blue Hawaiian

0803.description: Light in a glass thread, the cyan glow of data moving.

0804.name: Treasured Teal
0804.description: Teal kept in the good cupboard.

0806.description: The American robin's famously turquoise eggs; biliverdin does the colouring.

0808.name: Light Show

0810.name: Glacial Cyan
0810.description: Compressed-ice cyan from a crevasse wall.

0812.name: Splash
0812.description: The 1984 mermaid film and the sound, both this blue.

0813.name: Airglow

0814.description: Ice with the current on, glacial cyan overclocked.

0815.name: Pale Turquoise
0815.description: The X11 pastel of the gem-stone blue, turquoise in its morning robe.

0839.description: Grey-blue weather you save money for.
